metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the baseline for AC4's `git diff --name-only` command (e.g., `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD`) so the command actually verifies no core module was touched anywhere in the story branch.
evidence: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`' — no baseline ref is given.
Without a baseline, `git diff --name-only` (no args) compares working tree to index and would pass on a clean working tree even if the branch already modified core files.
findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` proving command lacks an explicit baseline reference, leaving the executor to guess what to diff against (HEAD~1? pre-story main? a named tag?). Without a baseline, the command can silently pass even if core files were touched in an earlier commit of the same branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'No core differ/apply module is modified by this story — verified by `git diff --name-only`...'
scenarios: A core file is modified in an earlier commit of the feature branch; `git diff --name-only` (no args or against HEAD) shows nothing and passes, falsely satisfying AC4.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: 'verified by `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD | grep -v -E "^(src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend\.py|src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend\.py|tests/)"` producing no output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `TicketTransport`'s transition method maps to `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status)` (the instance method), not the module-level `acli.transition_issue`, and note that `JiraBackend` should delegate to `self._client.transition_issue_by_name(...)` to keep the injected-transport contract testable.
evidence: acli.py:122 — `def transition_issue(jira_key: str, status: str)` is a module-level function that calls `resolve_jira_settings()` to self-construct an AcliClient; it is NOT a method on AcliClient.
acli.py:481 — `def transition_issue_by_name(self, jira_key: str, target_status: str) -> None` is the actual AcliClient instance method.
acli.py:146 — the module-level function delegates to client.transition_issue_by_name internally.
Plan asserts: 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls, so JiraBackend is a pure delegation shim'
findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient`, but `transition_issue` is a module-level function in `acli.py` that instantiates its own AcliClient using `resolve_jira_settings()` — it is NOT an AcliClient instance method. The actual AcliClient method is `transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status)`. Because the plan says Protocol signatures are 'derived from the de-facto surface the core already calls', the TicketTransport Protocol's transition method signature and name are under-specified: the implementor cannot determine whether to model the module-level function signature or the instance method signature.
findings.location: Approach section: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
scenarios: JiraBackend implements TicketTransport.transition(jira_key, status) by delegating to AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name — correct, but the plan does not specify this.

An implementor reads the plan, sees 'transition methods on acli.AcliClient', searches for `def transition` on AcliClient, finds `transition_issue_by_name` with a different signature than the module-level function, and must guess which to model.

If TicketTransport is modeled on the module-level function's signature, JiraBackend would need to call the module-level function, which self-instantiates a client using environment credentials — breaking the injected-transport pattern the contract suite relies on.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'transition methods on acli.AcliClient' with 'AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status) — the instance method (not the module-level acli.transition_issue, which self-constructs a client from env)' to pin the correct delegation target.

findings.finding: The plan attributes `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `OutboundMapper` ('OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'), but a full read of `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (outbound_fields.py:112-198) and a search of outbound_fields.py for 'fit_text_to_adf' confirm the function is never called there. `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is called from `_diff_fields` (outbound_fields.py:567) — the field-comparison path, not the mapping path. Attributing it to OutboundMapper is a false premise: the Protocol's method surface derived from the de-facto call sites would not include `fit_text_to_adf_limit` under the mapping role.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `(+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)` from the `OutboundMapper` derivation in the Approach section. If ADF truncation must be part of the backend interface, assign it to the role Protocol whose de-facto surface includes `_diff_fields` (likely a combined OutboundMapper that wraps both map + diff, or a separate FieldNormalizer role), and update the contract test assertion accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan places `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` under `OutboundMapper` (`OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)`), but the actual codebase shows `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is NOT called inside `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — it is called from `_diff_fields` (the differ/comparison path at `outbound_fields.py:567`) and from `inbound_differ.py:192`. The plan's own stated derivation principle is to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls', which would place `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in `FieldSanitizer` (already backed by `_sanitize_description`, which itself calls `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), not in `OutboundMapper`. Misattributing it to `OutboundMapper` would produce a Protocol that doesn't match actual call-site boundaries, complicating the S4 rewiring where sites call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` via the FieldSanitizer path (description send-side truncation) rather than the mapper path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: `OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` (remove `+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`); add `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `FieldSanitizer` alongside `_sanitize_description` (which already delegates to it), consistent with `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18` importing it for that purpose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `IdentityConvention.parse()` accepts both the canonical colon form (`rebar-id:<id>`) and the legacy hyphen form (`rebar-id-<id>`) — grounding the decision in the 7 read-path sites in the codebase that tolerate both — and add a contract test asserting the chosen behavior for the legacy form (even if the result is `None`/`RemoteRef`).
evidence: binding_walk.py:341: `for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-"):` — both forms tolerated in identity detection
binding_store.py:526: `hyphen_label = f"rebar-id-{local_id}"` — legacy-hyphen search still active
outbound_differ.py:302: `_EXCLUDED_PREFIXES: tuple[str, ...] = ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-", ...)` — both forms excluded from outbound diffs
apply_inbound.py:324: `if not isinstance(label, str) or not label.startswith("rebar-id-")` — hyphen-form removal still live

Plan is silent on whether IdentityConvention.parse() accepts/rejects the legacy hyphen form or returns None/raises — this is an interface decision that must be made at S2 when the contract is pinned, not deferred to S4
findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` Protocol's parse interface, as described in the plan, models only the canonical colon form (`rebar-id:{remote_id}`) but the actual codebase identity convention is a TWO-FORMAT system: all read paths (binding_walk.py:341, differ.py:498, outbound_differ.py:302, inbound_differ.py:251, apply_inbound.py:324, binding_store.py:526) must tolerate BOTH `rebar-id:<id>` (colon) and `rebar-id-<id>` (hyphen legacy) forms. If `IdentityConvention.parse()` only handles the colon form, the S4 rewire of those read sites through the convention object will require a signature change (breaking the S2-pinned contract) or will silently misidentify legacy-form labels as unbound (regression).
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention definition; plan phrase: 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'
scenarios: S4 rewires binding_walk.py through IdentityConvention.parse() — legacy `rebar-id-<id>` labels on existing Jira issues are not recognized as bound, causing re-creates
S2 contract test only exercises colon-form round-trip; the parse interface is pinned as colon-only; S4 must break the contract to add legacy-form tolerance
findings.suggested_fix: Define `IdentityConvention.parse(label: str) -> RemoteRef | None` to return a `RemoteRef` for BOTH `rebar-id:<id>` and `rebar-id-<id>` prefixes (since all codebase read paths tolerate both), and add a contract test asserting `parse("rebar-id-<id>")` returns a valid `RemoteRef`. Alternatively, explicitly document that the Protocol's `parse()` covers the canonical form only and that the legacy-form tolerance in S4 stays in the call sites (not routed through `IdentityConvention`), so the scope boundary is unambiguous.

findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` Protocol is specified to both stamp (`RemoteRef → label string`) and parse (`label string → RemoteRef`), but the label format encodes only `remote_id` (confirmed in codebase: `rebar-id:{local_id}`), making `vendor` and `instance` unrecoverable from the label string alone. The rule for reconstructing those `RemoteRef` fields during parse is never specified.
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention description; Testing section — 'parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip'
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that `IdentityConvention` is constructed with `vendor` and `instance` as fixed constants (e.g., `JiraIdentityConvention(vendor='jira', instance=<instance_url>)`), so `parse` uses those injected values to fill the non-encoded `RemoteRef` fields, and document this in the Protocol's method signature.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC4's permitted-file list to include `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (and any other package init files) that must be updated when introducing new modules, so the `git diff` check does not falsely flag necessary packaging changes.
evidence: AC4 lists only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` as permitted files.

The IdentityConvention object is also new under `adapters/jira/` (per the plan: 'S2 introduces `IdentityConvention` as a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`'), which may need an `__init__.py` entry.
findings.finding: AC4's allowed-file list (`_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, files under `tests/`) may be too narrow: introducing a new module under `adapters/jira/` likely requires updating `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (or `adapters/__init__.py`), which are not listed as permitted touches. The AC would then incorrectly flag a valid `__init__.py` update as a violation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
scenarios: Executor adds `adapters/jira/identity.py` for IdentityConvention and updates `adapters/jira/__init__.py` to export it — AC4's diff check flags `__init__.py` as an unexpected touch, creating a false failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, `adapters/jira/__init__.py` (package wiring only), and files under `tests/`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add contract test cases for IdentityConvention covering: (a) parsing the legacy 'rebar-id-' prefix variant (accept or reject — but make it explicit); (b) a malformed/unrecognized label passed to parse; (c) a RemoteRef whose remote_id contains special characters; (d) an empty-string label.
evidence: 'The S2 contract test exercises the convention object directly (stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip), needing no transport and touching no core module.' — only happy-path coverage specified.

The plan notes the existing inlined logic handles two prefix variants ('rebar-id:' and 'rebar-id-'), but does not specify which the new IdentityConvention must accept, nor whether it should reject the legacy variant.

No test is specified for: malformed label (no id after prefix), unrelated label passed to parse, empty string, the legacy 'rebar-id-' prefix variant, or a RemoteRef with special characters in remote_id.
findings.finding: The IdentityConvention is new parsing logic (not a delegation shim), but the test plan specifies only the happy-path round-trip (stamp RemoteRef → label, parse label → RemoteRef) with no failure or edge-case paths, despite the plan body noting the existing code handles both 'rebar-id:' and 'rebar-id-' prefix variants that the new object must absorb or explicitly reject.
findings.location: Testing section: 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'; Approach section: 'the read is the "rebar-id:"/"rebar-id-" prefix scan at binding_walk.py:341'
scenarios: binding_walk.py currently recognizes 'rebar-id-' labels; if IdentityConvention.parse only handles 'rebar-id:' and S4 routes binding_walk through it, existing tickets with legacy labels silently fail to bind.
A remote_id containing a colon or delimiter character causes IdentityConvention.parse to misidentify the boundary — undetected without a malformed-input test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the file-home ambiguity for the IdentityConvention implementation: either name its file explicitly in the edit-set and update AC #4's git-diff list to include it, or explicitly state it lives inside adapters/jira/backend.py so the 'self-contained' language is clearly about logical encapsulation, not a separate file.
evidence: "S2 introduces IdentityConvention as a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/"
AC #4: "verified by git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/"
The edit-set in Context also lists only two production files, but does not name a file for the IdentityConvention implementation object.
findings.finding: AC #4 lists exactly two production-code files ('_backend.py' and 'adapters/jira/backend.py') as the expected git diff output, but the Approach section describes IdentityConvention as 'a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/' — language that implies it could be a separate file (e.g. adapters/jira/identity_convention.py) rather than embedded in adapters/jira/backend.py. If an implementer reads the Approach literally and creates a third file, the AC #4 verification command will spuriously fail; if they embed it in backend.py to satisfy AC #4, the 'self-contained' language is misleading.
findings.location: Approach section ('a new, self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/') vs. AC #4 ('git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/')
scenarios: Implementer creates adapters/jira/identity_convention.py as a separate file (reasonable given 'self-contained'), causing the AC #4 git-diff check to fail.
Implementer embeds IdentityConvention in backend.py to pass AC #4, but the 'self-contained' framing of the Approach is then misleading and the plan appears internally inconsistent.
findings.suggested_fix: In the edit-set, add a third entry: 'New `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` — the `JiraIdentityConvention` class implementing `IdentityConvention`.' Then update AC #4's git-diff check to include this path. Alternatively, if the intent is to embed it in backend.py, replace 'self-contained pure convention object under adapters/jira/' with 'pure convention class inside adapters/jira/backend.py'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `SupportsIncremental` has at least one current call site or is required by an AC in this story/S3/S4 — if not, defer it to the story that first introduces an incremental fetch consumer (satisfying YAGNI/Rule-of-Three).
evidence: grep for 'incremental' across all reconciler source returns zero hits
The fetcher (`fetcher.py`) always performs a full snapshot scan with no incremental/delta path
No AC line, no linked ticket (S3/S4/S5), and no epic `be74` description names an incremental fetch capability

The plan's own YAGNI justification for `SupportsLinks`/`SupportsComments` ('observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links') has no parallel evidence for `SupportsIncremental`
Absence: zero test AC or contract test assertion references `SupportsIncremental`
findings.finding: `SupportsIncremental` is a YAGNI opt-in capability Protocol with zero existing call sites in the codebase — no incremental fetch mode exists, no current done-definition requires it, and no consumer in this story or its dependents (S3/S4) exercises an `isinstance(backend, SupportsIncremental)` guard.
findings.location: Context / Edit-set: `_backend.py` — 'three opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`)'
scenarios: A second backend author reads the Protocol surface and implements an incremental path that never gets called because no core consumer guards on `SupportsIncremental`.
The protocol definition locks the interface prematurely before the incremental fetch design is known, making it harder to change when the real requirement is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `SupportsIncremental` from `_backend.py` in this story. Add it when the first core consumer that guards on `isinstance(backend, SupportsIncremental)` is introduced (likely a future fetcher story). The contract suite and `FakeBackend` have nothing to test for it today.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the precedent citation: note that `@runtime_checkable` for structural injection exists at `outbound_differ.py:228`, but isinstance-based capability opt-in dispatch is *new* in this story; also note that `apply_outbound.py:212` uses a `hasattr` guard (a related pattern) that S4 will migrate to isinstance.
evidence: outbound_differ.py:228-233: '@runtime_checkable class BindingStoreProtocol(Protocol): """Minimal interface for the binding store (PR #401)."""'
inbound_differ.py:78-82: '@runtime_checkable class BindingStoreProtocol(Protocol): ...'
apply_outbound.py:212: 'if client is not None and hasattr(client, "add_comment"):' — existing capability guard uses hasattr, not isinstance
No existing isinstance(x, SupportsLinks/SupportsComments/SupportsIncremental) pattern found in repo
findings.finding: The plan cites `outbound_differ.py:228` as the established `@runtime_checkable` precedent for capability-detection via isinstance, but line 228 is `BindingStoreProtocol` — a store-interface protocol used for structural injection, not a capability opt-in guard. The actual capability-detection pattern (isinstance-guarded dispatch on an opt-in Protocol) has no prior precedent in the codebase; it is new behavior introduced by this story, not an established pattern being replicated.
findings.location: Edit-set section, _backend.py description: 'which is already the reconciler's established pattern (outbound_differ.py:228, inbound_differ.py)'
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan assumes isinstance-based capability dispatch already works this way in the codebase and skips validating the pattern — then discovers the existing guard at apply_outbound.py:212 uses hasattr, not isinstance, which contradicts the plan's framing.

findings.finding: The plan enumerates 'four core call sites' for the rebar-id label convention (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223, binding_walk.py:341), but grep reveals at least two additional label-formatting sites: binding_store.py:522-523 (formats 'rebar-id:{local_id}' as both a label string and a JQL clause) and dispatch_one.py:237 (formats 'rebar-id:{local_id}' inside a JQL string). The IdentityConvention object introduced by S2 is described as covering stamp→label and parse←label, but the JQL-embedding call sites are not mentioned. If S4 routes all rebar-id: formatting through IdentityConvention, the interface as designed may need a jql_clause(ref) method — omitting these sites now risks a gap when S4 attempts full routing.
findings.location: Approach section — 'IdentityConvention' paragraph; binding_store.py:522-523, dispatch_one.py:237
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the IdentityConvention description: 'also covers `jql_clause(ref) -> str` returning the JQL-safe label predicate (e.g. `labels = "rebar-id:{remote_id}"`)' and update the call-site inventory from 4 to 6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the structural form of `Backend` in `_backend.py` (e.g., 'Backend is a `@runtime_checkable Protocol` that inherits from the five role Protocols, so `JiraBackend` need only implement all their methods') so an executor does not have to infer the relationship.
evidence: 'New `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py` — the `Backend` facade, the five role Protocols...' — Backend is listed separately from the five role Protocols but its structural relationship to them is not defined.
AC: '`JiraBackend` implements `Backend`' implies Backend is an interface, but the interface shape is unspecified.
findings.finding: The `Backend` facade's structural nature is not specified: the plan names it separately from the five role Protocols but does not state whether it is a `@runtime_checkable Protocol` that inherits/composes the five role Protocols, an abstract base class, a plain class that accepts role-protocol instances as constructor arguments, or something else. An executor implementing `JiraBackend` must guess the structural relationship between `Backend` and the five role Protocols.
findings.location: Edit-set bullet: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py`
scenarios: Executor implements Backend as a Protocol composing the five role Protocols, but the contract suite authors it as an ABC — type-checker passes one and fails the other.
Executor makes Backend a dataclass holding role-protocol instances (composition over inheritance), which changes how JiraBackend is constructed vs. the delegation shim described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Edit-set bullet: 'Backend is itself a @runtime_checkable Protocol that directly inherits from TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, and IdentityConvention, so JiraBackend satisfies isinstance(jira_backend, Backend) by implementing all five role-protocol method sets in a single class.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring either a new ADR (or an explicit amendment to ADR 0035) that documents the `Backend` facade structure, the five role + three capability Protocols, and the rationale for `isinstance`-based capability detection over a `capabilities()` method — so the decision is traceable after the story closes.
evidence: 'the Backend facade, its role Protocols, and the RemoteRef identity type pinned in ADR 0035 §(d)' — ADR 0035 is referenced for RemoteRef only, not for the facade or capability-detection design

'Capability-detection mechanism (pinned here): callers … detect an opt-in capability with isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)… There is no capabilities() method.' — a deliberate design choice with no ADR home
No Acceptance Criterion requires an ADR update or new design-doc entry
T5e (c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — not satisfied by story text alone
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — the `Backend` facade, the `isinstance`-based `@runtime_checkable` capability-detection mechanism (explicitly chosen over a `capabilities()` method), five role Protocols, three opt-in capability Protocols, and the contract test suite pattern — but does not call for updating ADR 0035 or authoring a new ADR to capture this design and its rationale. ADR 0035 §(d) is cited only for `RemoteRef` pinning; the broader facade + capability-detection design lives only in the story body and will be untracked once the story closes.
findings.location: Context / Problem section and Approach section; Acceptance Criteria (no ADR-update AC)
scenarios: A future engineer adding a third backend searches ADRs for the capability-detection contract, finds nothing, and re-invents or misreads the pattern.
A reviewer later asks 'why isinstance and not capabilities()?' and there is no authoritative answer in the decision record.
ADR 0035 remains incomplete as the canonical reference for the Backend abstraction, causing confusion between what §(d) covers and what this story adds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an Acceptance Criterion: 'ADR 0035 is amended (or a new ADR-0036 is created) to document the `Backend` facade, the role/capability Protocol split, and the `isinstance`-based capability-detection rationale. The ADR diff is included in the PR and `make lint` confirms no stale references to the old §(d)-only scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that characterization test expected values are **independently authored** (hand-written from the spec or reviewed as authoritative), not machine-generated snapshots of current output — and add an AC note that the test author must verify each expected value is *correct*, not merely *current*.
evidence: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty).'

The plan does not specify that expected values are independently authored from first principles or reviewed for correctness — 'captured from current behavior' implies running the pre-story code and recording its output as the oracle.
If expected values are auto-generated snapshots, any bug in _map_local_to_jira_fields is locked in rather than caught.
findings.finding: The characterization test AC specifies that expected values are 'captured from current behavior' and verified by 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty,' which describes the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: if expected values are machine-generated from the current codebase output (e.g., snapshot update tooling), they enshrine any existing bugs in the mappers and cannot catch pre-existing incorrectness — they only verify the delegation did not add NEW divergence.
findings.location: AC: Characterization tests / Testing section: 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs… verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior'
scenarios: A mapper has a latent bug (e.g., wrong priority key). Characterization test captures the buggy output as expected. JiraBackend delegates correctly. Test passes. Bug is invisible — and now pinned by a test that will resist fixing it.

A future story attempts to fix the mapper output; the characterization test goes RED as intended, but reviewers treat it as a regression rather than a fix — because the oracle has no independent authority.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' with 'literal expected values authored from the field-mapping specification (or reviewed for correctness by the implementer), not generated by running the current code.' Require the test author to annotate any value that is 'current behavior, correctness unverified' so it can be cleaned up in a follow-on story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in AC#4 (or in the Scope section) that `adapters/jira/backend.py` is a shared edit target with S3 (which adds `@register`), so the isolation `git diff` gate should be read as S2-only branch scope.
evidence: Plan AC#4: 'verified by git diff --name-only touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/'
S3 file_impact entry: {path: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py', reason: 'Register JiraBackend under key jira'}
S2 edit-set: 'New src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py — JiraBackend, a thin wrapper...'
findings.finding: adapters/jira/backend.py appears in BOTH this story's edit-set AND S3's file_impact (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 lists it to add @register decoration), meaning the AC#4 git-diff isolation check ('touching only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and files under tests/') will be trivially satisfied on S2's own branch but the file is a shared edit target across two stories — the overlap is unacknowledged and could cause merge friction or mislead the isolation assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #4 / Edit-set section / S3 ticket 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 file_impact
scenarios: If S2 and S3 land on the same base branch, the S3 author may not realize S2 already created the file and needs to edit (not create) it.

The AC#4 isolation gate says 'touching only' these files — if the reviewer runs it on S3's branch, adapters/jira/backend.py appears there too but for a different reason, causing confusion about which story owns the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to AC#4: 'Note: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) will subsequently edit adapters/jira/backend.py to add @register("jira", …); this story creates the file and S3 amends it — both stories satisfy the gate independently on their own branches.'

findings.finding: The 'Teeth' mutation-test step (perturb a JiraBackend delegation, confirm RED, restore) is described in the Testing section but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, so there is no proving command that verifies the contract suite would actually catch a regression.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)

findings.finding: The Testing section describes a mandatory 'Teeth' step — 'perturb a `JiraBackend` delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this was performed, leaving it as undisprovable guidance rather than a checkable gate.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' paragraph) — no corresponding AC

findings.finding: The 'Teeth' process step — 'perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — is described work in the Testing section with no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it was performed.
findings.location: Testing section, 'Teeth' subsection; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)

findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) are named and declared `@runtime_checkable`, but their method surfaces (the method names and signatures a backend must implement to satisfy the `isinstance()` check) are never enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — capability Protocol list; _backend.py edit-set description

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
